items.q: Why did the Treaty of Versailles cause resentment in Germany?
items.correct: It assigned sole war guilt to Germany (Article 231) and imposed harsh reparations, territorial losses and military restrictions.
items.misconception: Because Germany lost more soldiers than any other country in the war.
items.random_wrong: Because Germany was not invited to sign it.
items.why: Students conflate the harshness of the treaty with battlefield losses. Germany WAS excluded from negotiations, but the resentment was about the terms (especially the war-guilt clause) — not exclusion from signing.

items.q: What caused the First World War?
items.correct: Long-term tensions (militarism, alliances, imperialism, nationalism) combined with the July Crisis triggered by the assassination of Franz Ferdinand.
items.misconception: The assassination of Archduke Franz Ferdinand caused the war.
items.random_wrong: Germany declared war on Britain to seize colonies.
items.why: The assassination was the trigger, not the cause. Students treat the spark as sufficient explanation, ignoring the systemic pressures (MAIN factors) that made a wider war possible.

items.q: Why did Britain pursue appeasement in the 1930s?
items.correct: Britain was militarily unprepared, traumatised by WWI losses, sympathetic to revising Versailles, and feared communism more than Nazism.
items.misconception: Because Chamberlain was a coward and admired Hitler.
items.random_wrong: Because Britain had no army at all in the 1930s.
items.why: Hindsight makes appeasement look like weakness. In context it was a rational (if mistaken) strategy reflecting public opinion, financial constraints and genuine fear of another total war.

items.q: What caused the Cold War?
items.correct: Ideological incompatibility plus mutual suspicion after WWII — disputes over Eastern Europe, the atomic bomb, and broken wartime agreements.
items.misconception: Stalin started it by invading Eastern Europe after the war.
items.random_wrong: It began when the USSR shot down a US spy plane.
items.why: Students treat one side as solely responsible. Most historians now see the Cold War as a mutual breakdown — both sides acted on rational fears that the other read as aggression.

items.q: What happened in the Russian Revolution of 1917?
items.correct: There were two revolutions: February overthrew the Tsar and installed the Provisional Government; October saw the Bolsheviks seize power from that government.
items.misconception: The Bolsheviks overthrew the Tsar in 1917.
items.random_wrong: Lenin personally led the storming of the Winter Palace.
items.why: Students collapse 1917 into one event. The Tsar abdicated in February under pressure from liberals and soldiers — the Bolsheviks took power eight months later from a republican government.

items.q: Why did Henry VIII break with Rome?
items.correct: Primarily for an annulment from Catherine of Aragon, but also to seize Church wealth and assert royal supremacy — not for Protestant theological reasons.
items.misconception: Because he wanted England to become Protestant.
items.random_wrong: Because the Pope excommunicated him for marrying Anne Boleyn.
items.why: Henry remained doctrinally Catholic to his death. The break was political and dynastic; Protestantism only took hold in England under Edward VI and Elizabeth I.

items.q: Why was the Spanish Armada defeated in 1588?
items.correct: A combination of English tactics and fireships, poor Spanish planning, and the 'Protestant wind' storms that wrecked the fleet on the return.
items.misconception: Drake defeated the Armada single-handedly in a great sea battle.
items.random_wrong: Elizabeth I personally commanded the English fleet at sea.
items.why: Drake is mythologised. The English fleet harassed but did not destroy the Armada in battle — most Spanish ships were lost to storms around Scotland and Ireland.

items.q: How did Hitler come to power in 1933?
items.correct: He was legally appointed Chancellor by President Hindenburg as part of a conservative political deal, after the Nazis became the largest party in the Reichstag.
items.misconception: Hitler seized power through a violent coup or revolution.
items.random_wrong: Hitler won an outright majority in the 1932 presidential election.
items.why: The 1923 Beer Hall Putsch failed; in 1933 Hitler came to power constitutionally. Students confuse Nazi violence with the actual mechanism of takeover, which was legal manoeuvring.

items.q: When did the systematic mass murder of Jews begin?
items.correct: Mass shootings by Einsatzgruppen began with Operation Barbarossa in June 1941; industrial extermination camps followed from 1942.
items.misconception: The Holocaust began as soon as Hitler came to power in 1933.
items.random_wrong: The killings only began after Germany lost the Battle of Stalingrad.
items.why: Persecution began in 1933, but systematic murder only started in 1941 with the invasion of the USSR. Students conflate persecution, ghettoisation and extermination as a single early policy.

items.q: How was the US civil rights movement achieved?
items.correct: Through decades of mass organising — NAACP legal work, grassroots campaigns, sit-ins, freedom rides — alongside leaders like King and Malcolm X.
items.misconception: Martin Luther King's 'I Have a Dream' speech ended segregation.
items.random_wrong: Lincoln ended segregation by signing the 14th Amendment.
items.why: Students reduce the movement to one leader and one speech. The 1964 Civil Rights Act resulted from sustained pressure by thousands of activists, not a single moment of inspiration.

items.q: What caused the French Revolution of 1789?
items.correct: Financial crisis from war debts, an unfair tax system burdening the Third Estate, Enlightenment ideas, and royal mismanagement of the Estates-General.
items.misconception: The starving poor stormed the Bastille and overthrew the king.
items.random_wrong: Napoleon led the revolution to replace the Bourbon monarchy.
items.why: Students see the revolution as a peasant uprising. In fact it was led by middle-class lawyers and professionals (the bourgeoisie); food riots were a symptom, not the engine.

items.q: Why did the Industrial Revolution begin in Britain?
items.correct: A combination of capital from trade and empire, coal and iron deposits, agricultural improvements, political stability, and a culture of invention.
items.misconception: Because James Watt invented the steam engine.
items.random_wrong: Because Britain banned slavery before other countries.
items.why: Inventions are effects of the conditions, not causes. Steam engines existed before Watt; what mattered was the economic and social environment that made them profitable to deploy at scale.

items.q: Why did Britain abolish slavery?
items.correct: A combination of moral campaigning, slave resistance and rebellions (especially Haiti and Jamaica), and the shifting economic interest of industrial capitalism.
items.misconception: Wilberforce single-handedly persuaded Parliament to end slavery out of pure morality.
items.random_wrong: Slavery was abolished because cotton was no longer profitable.
items.why: Students inherit the heroic Wilberforce narrative. Recent scholarship emphasises slave revolts (Haiti, Demerara, Jamaica) and economic shifts — abolition was not a gift from above.

items.q: Why did the British Empire end after WWII?
items.correct: Britain was bankrupted by the war, faced rising nationalist movements, and confronted US/Soviet pressure against colonialism in a new world order.
items.misconception: Britain voluntarily gave its colonies independence as a moral choice.
items.random_wrong: The UN forced Britain to grant independence to all colonies in 1945.
items.why: The 'benevolent withdrawal' narrative is a national myth. Britain was forced out by financial collapse and nationalist pressure — often violently, as in Kenya, Malaya and Cyprus.

items.q: Why did Britain win the Battle of Britain?
items.correct: Radar, the Dowding command system, home advantage in pilot recovery, German tactical errors (switching to bombing cities), and adequate fighter production.
items.misconception: The RAF won because Spitfires were better than any German aircraft.
items.random_wrong: Hitler called off the attack to invade the USSR immediately.
items.why: The Bf 109 was comparable to the Spitfire. Victory came from integrated air defence and German strategic mistakes, not aircraft superiority — a key point in modern historiography.

items.q: How was the Cuban Missile Crisis resolved?
items.correct: A secret deal: the USSR withdrew missiles from Cuba; the US publicly pledged not to invade Cuba and secretly agreed to remove Jupiter missiles from Turkey.
items.misconception: Kennedy stared Khrushchev down and the Soviets backed off unconditionally.
items.random_wrong: The crisis ended when Castro persuaded Khrushchev to withdraw.
items.why: The Turkey concession was kept secret for decades, fuelling the myth of unilateral US victory. The actual resolution was a negotiated compromise — important for understanding nuclear diplomacy.

items.q: Why did Stalin launch the Great Terror?
items.correct: A combination of genuine paranoia, removal of political rivals, consolidation of personal power, and the dynamics of a self-reinforcing security apparatus.
items.misconception: Stalin was simply mentally ill and killed people at random.
items.random_wrong: The Terror was a response to a planned coup by Trotsky.
items.why: Reducing the Terror to madness ignores its political logic. It served real functions — eliminating the old Bolsheviks, replacing them with loyalists, and terrorising society into compliance.

items.q: Why did William of Normandy win the Battle of Hastings?
items.correct: Harold's exhausted army had just marched south after defeating the Norwegians at Stamford Bridge; William used feigned retreats and combined cavalry, infantry and archers.
items.misconception: Harold lost because he was killed by an arrow in the eye early in the battle.
items.random_wrong: William won because he had gunpowder and the English did not.
items.why: The arrow story comes from the Bayeux Tapestry and may not be literal. The battle was close all day; the real factors were Harold's tired army and William's tactical flexibility.

items.q: What were the long-term effects of the Black Death?
items.correct: Labour shortages strengthened peasants' bargaining power, weakened serfdom in England, contributed to the Peasants' Revolt, and reshaped religious authority.
items.misconception: The Black Death wiped out medieval society and ended the Middle Ages overnight.
items.random_wrong: The Black Death was caused by witches and was stopped by burning them.
items.why: The plague was catastrophic but medieval society continued. Its importance lies in slow structural changes (wages, labour, religion) — not in being a single 'ending' event.

items.q: Why did the US lose the Vietnam War?
items.correct: A determined nationalist insurgency, unwinnable counter-insurgency conditions, collapsing political support at home, and a corrupt South Vietnamese ally.
items.misconception: The US lost because the media turned the public against the war.
items.random_wrong: The US lost because it was outnumbered and outgunned militarily.
items.why: The 'media stab in the back' is a popular but contested claim. The US had overwhelming firepower; the deeper problem was political — fighting a nationalist movement with an unpopular client state.

items.q: What caused the German hyperinflation of 1923?
items.correct: The government printing money to pay striking workers during the French occupation of the Ruhr, on top of existing reparations and war debts.
items.misconception: Reparations alone caused the hyperinflation.
items.random_wrong: The hyperinflation was caused by the Wall Street Crash.
items.why: Reparations contributed but the immediate trigger was the passive resistance in the Ruhr funded by printing money. Students treat reparations as a sufficient cause when they were one factor among several.

items.q: What did the Munich Agreement actually achieve?
items.correct: It transferred the Sudetenland to Germany without Czech consent, bought time for British rearmament, and emboldened Hitler to seize the rest of Czechoslovakia six months later.
items.misconception: It was a complete failure that achieved nothing.
items.random_wrong: It returned the Sudetenland to Czechoslovakia.
items.why: Revisionist historians argue Munich gave Britain crucial rearmament time. Students see it as pure capitulation; the picture is more complex — it failed strategically but had short-term benefits.

items.q: Why did the American colonies rebel against Britain?
items.correct: Disputes over taxation without representation after the Seven Years' War, growing colonial identity, and British attempts to tighten imperial control on previously autonomous colonies.
items.misconception: The colonists rebelled because they were heavily oppressed and impoverished.
items.random_wrong: Because Britain banned them from owning land west of the Atlantic.
items.why: Americans were actually among the wealthiest and least-taxed people in the empire. The revolt was about principle and political control, not material suffering — a key counter-intuitive point.

items.q: What caused the English Civil War?
items.correct: Long-term tensions over royal absolutism, religion (Laudian reforms), and parliamentary rights, combined with Charles I's specific mismanagement and personal rigidity.
items.misconception: It was caused by Charles I trying to arrest five MPs in 1642.
items.random_wrong: It was caused by Henry VIII's break with Rome.
items.why: The arrest attempt was the trigger; the causes were structural. Students mistake the proximate event for the underlying religious-political crisis that had built for decades.

items.q: Why was India partitioned in 1947?
items.correct: The Muslim League's demand for Pakistan, Congress refusal of power-sharing, communal violence, British haste to leave, and Mountbatten's accelerated timetable.
items.misconception: Hindus and Muslims had always hated each other so partition was inevitable.
items.random_wrong: Gandhi insisted on partition to ensure Hindu dominance.
items.why: The 'ancient hatreds' explanation is ahistorical — Hindu-Muslim politics had been negotiable into the 1930s. Partition was the product of specific political failures, not timeless religious hostility.

items.q: Why did Japan attack Pearl Harbor?
items.correct: To cripple the US Pacific Fleet and buy time to secure resource-rich Southeast Asia after the US oil embargo threatened Japanese expansion.
items.misconception: Japan attacked because it wanted to conquer America.
items.random_wrong: Japan attacked to support Germany's invasion of the USSR.
items.why: Japan never planned to conquer the US — the goal was a defensive perimeter. Students assume the attack signalled invasion ambitions when it was a desperate strategic gamble.

items.q: Why did the First Crusade happen?
items.correct: A mix of Byzantine appeals for help, papal ambition to reunite Christendom under Rome, religious enthusiasm, land hunger among younger sons, and indulgences offered to fighters.
items.misconception: Christians launched the Crusades to convert Muslims to Christianity.
items.random_wrong: Crusaders went to find the Holy Grail.
items.why: Forced conversion was not the aim; recapturing holy sites and aiding fellow Christians was. Students project later missionary motives onto medieval warfare which had different goals.

items.q: Why did British women get the vote in 1918?
items.correct: A combination of pre-war suffragist and suffragette campaigning, women's contribution to WWI, and broader political changes including extending male suffrage.
items.misconception: Women got the vote as a reward for their work in WWI.
items.random_wrong: Emmeline Pankhurst was elected to Parliament and voted it through.
items.why: The 'WWI reward' story is popular but only partial — older property-owning women got the vote, not the young factory workers. Pre-war campaigning and political pragmatism mattered as much.

items.q: Was Kristallnacht a spontaneous uprising?
items.correct: No — it was orchestrated by Goebbels and the Nazi Party using the assassination of a German diplomat as a pretext for coordinated state-sanctioned violence.
items.misconception: It was a spontaneous outburst of public anger against Jews.
items.random_wrong: Hitler personally led the attacks on synagogues.
items.why: Nazi propaganda framed it as spontaneous; documents prove it was organised. Students take the regime's framing at face value, missing how authoritarian violence is staged as popular will.

items.q: Why did the Communists win the Chinese Civil War?
items.correct: Mao's appeal to peasants through land reform, the discrediting of the KMT by corruption and inflation, and the disciplined organisation of the PLA — not foreign aid.
items.misconception: Mao won because the USSR sent the Red Army to help.
items.random_wrong: Mao won because the USA refused to support the KMT.
items.why: Soviet aid was limited and often unhelpful; the US heavily backed the KMT. The Communist victory was won domestically through rural mobilisation — a key historiographical point.

items.q: Why did William of Normandy claim the English throne in 1066?
items.correct: He claimed Edward the Confessor had promised him the throne in 1051 and that Harold Godwinson had sworn an oath to support that claim in 1064.
items.misconception: William invaded because he was the strongest king in Europe and wanted more land.
items.random_wrong: William was Edward the Confessor's eldest son.
items.why: Students treat the invasion as raw conquest, missing that William framed it as enforcing a legitimate hereditary and oath-based claim — legitimacy mattered hugely in 11th-century politics.

items.q: Why did William the Conqueror commission the Domesday Book in 1086?
items.correct: To record landholding and resources so he could assess taxation (geld), confirm Norman tenants' rights, and settle disputes over confiscated Anglo-Saxon estates.
items.misconception: It was a census of the English population, like a modern one.
items.random_wrong: It was a list of Anglo-Saxon rebels William wanted to execute.
items.why: Domesday counted land, livestock and ploughs, not people as individuals. Students project modern census ideas onto a fiscal-feudal record concerned with who owed what to whom.

items.q: How did the feudal system work in Norman England?
items.correct: The king granted land to tenants-in-chief in return for knight service; they in turn enfeoffed knights who held land in return for military service to their lord.
items.misconception: Feudalism meant peasants were slaves owned by the king.
items.random_wrong: Feudalism was a written constitution introduced by William in 1066.
items.why: Feudalism described a tenurial chain of obligations among free landholders, not slavery. Most peasants were villeins tied to manors — a related but separate system.

items.q: Why did Henry II clash with Thomas Becket?
items.correct: Becket resisted Henry's attempt to bring 'criminous clerks' under royal courts via the Constitutions of Clarendon, defending Church independence from royal jurisdiction.
items.misconception: Henry II ordered Becket's assassination personally.
items.random_wrong: They quarrelled because Becket wanted to be Pope.
items.why: Henry's outburst was rhetorical and he did penance after. The dispute was about jurisdiction over clergy, not personal hatred — students dramatise the murder and miss the constitutional issue.

items.q: What did Magna Carta actually do in 1215?
items.correct: It was a peace treaty between King John and rebel barons protecting baronial privileges and limiting arbitrary royal taxation and imprisonment — it was annulled within months.
items.misconception: Magna Carta gave ordinary English people democratic rights and freedom.
items.random_wrong: Magna Carta abolished the monarchy and created Parliament.
items.why: Magna Carta protected the elite, not commoners; villeins are barely mentioned. Its democratic reputation is a later (17th-century) reinterpretation projected backwards.

items.q: How did medieval people explain the Black Death?
items.correct: Most blamed God's punishment for sin or bad air (miasma); some scapegoated Jews; doctors blamed planetary alignments and imbalanced humours.
items.misconception: Medieval people knew it was spread by rats and fleas but had no way to stop it.
items.random_wrong: Medieval people thought it was caused by germs they couldn't see.
items.why: Germ theory did not exist until the 19th century. Students retroject modern knowledge onto medieval responses, missing the religious and humoral frameworks that shaped real behaviour.

items.q: What caused the Peasants' Revolt of 1381?
items.correct: The poll tax of 1381 on top of long-term anger over labour laws restricting peasant wages after the Black Death and resentment at clerical wealth.
items.misconception: The peasants revolted because they were starving and had no food.
items.random_wrong: The peasants wanted to make Wat Tyler king of England.
items.why: Post-plague wages were actually rising, which is why landlords tried to cap them. The revolt was about freedom and tax, not famine — a counter-intuitive point students miss.

items.q: What were the Wars of the Roses really about?
items.correct: Dynastic conflict between rival branches of the Plantagenets (Lancaster and York) over the throne, intensified by weak kingship under Henry VI and noble factionalism.
items.misconception: The whole country was at war for thirty continuous years.
items.random_wrong: They were a religious war between Catholics and Protestants.
items.why: Fighting was sporadic and most of England saw no battles; ordinary life carried on. Students imagine total civil war; in reality, it was an aristocratic dispute fought in short campaigns.

items.q: How did Henry VII secure the Tudor dynasty after 1485?
items.correct: Through marriage to Elizabeth of York, ruthless control of the nobility (bonds and recognisances), financial discipline, and crushing Yorkist pretenders like Simnel and Warbeck.
items.misconception: Henry VII was universally popular because he ended the Wars of the Roses.
items.random_wrong: Henry VII was Richard III's nephew and inherited the throne legally.
items.why: Henry's claim was weak; he ruled by control, not consent. Students assume the wars 'ended' at Bosworth, missing how shaky early Tudor rule was.

items.q: What was the Elizabethan Religious Settlement of 1559?
items.correct: A deliberate via media — a Protestant doctrine and English-language prayer book combined with some Catholic-style ceremony — designed to maximise conformity.
items.misconception: Elizabeth made England fully Protestant and banned Catholic worship outright.
items.random_wrong: Elizabeth reunited England with the Pope to make peace with Spain.
items.why: The settlement was a compromise designed to be broadly acceptable. Students assume binary Protestant/Catholic divides; Elizabeth deliberately blurred them to keep her realm stable.

items.q: Why did the Gunpowder Plotters try to blow up Parliament in 1605?
items.correct: Catholic gentlemen, frustrated that James I had not eased anti-Catholic laws as hoped, planned to kill the king and Lords and put a Catholic monarch on the throne.
items.misconception: Guy Fawkes planned and led the Gunpowder Plot.
items.random_wrong: The plot aimed to make England independent from the Pope.
items.why: The leader was Robert Catesby; Fawkes was the explosives expert. Students remember Fawkes because he was caught, but he was not the mastermind.

items.q: Was the English Civil War caused by religion alone?
items.correct: No — religion (Laudian reforms, Scottish Presbyterians) combined with financial disputes (ship money), political conflict over parliamentary rights, and Charles I's personal style.
items.misconception: The Civil War was a war of religion between Catholics and Protestants.
items.random_wrong: The Civil War began because Cromwell wanted to be king.
items.why: Both sides were largely Protestant. Reducing the war to religion misses the financial and constitutional disputes; modern historians stress multi-causal explanations.

items.q: What kind of ruler was Oliver Cromwell?
items.correct: A military dictator who governed as Lord Protector after dissolving Parliament, justified by Puritan religious mission, but unable to create a stable system that outlived him.
items.misconception: Cromwell was a democratic leader who replaced monarchy with rule by the people.
items.random_wrong: Cromwell was a Catholic priest who deposed Charles I.
items.why: Cromwell dissolved parliaments he disliked and ruled via the army. Students confuse anti-monarchism with democracy — the Interregnum was authoritarian, not popular rule.

items.q: Why was the monarchy restored in 1660?
items.correct: After Cromwell's death the Protectorate collapsed; General Monck negotiated Charles II's return on the terms of the Declaration of Breda (amnesty, religious flexibility, army pay).
items.misconception: Charles II invaded England with a French army and seized the throne.
items.random_wrong: Parliament voted to restore the monarchy because Cromwell had taxed them too heavily.
items.why: Charles returned by invitation, not invasion. Students imagine a violent reconquest; in fact, the political class restored him to escape military rule.

items.q: Why is 1688 called the Glorious Revolution?
items.correct: Parliament invited William of Orange to replace the Catholic James II, with little bloodshed in England, establishing parliamentary supremacy and the 1689 Bill of Rights.
items.misconception: It was glorious because no one died anywhere in the conflict.
items.random_wrong: William of Orange was Charles II's son and inherited the throne.
items.why: It was bloody in Ireland and Scotland (Boyne, Killiecrankie). Students take the English-centric label literally and miss the violent imposition of the new regime elsewhere.

items.q: Why did the factory system replace the domestic system?
items.correct: Water-powered then steam-powered machinery was too large and expensive for cottages; factories concentrated workers around machines, enabling discipline, supervision, and scale.
items.misconception: Factories appeared because steam engines were invented.
items.random_wrong: Factories appeared because the government banned home weaving.
items.why: The shift was about machinery scale and capital investment, not just invention. Domestic spinning continued; factories triumphed where mechanisation outscaled hand work.

items.q: Why were 19th-century industrial cities so unhealthy?
items.correct: Rapid population growth outpaced housing, water and sewage; lack of regulation produced overcrowded slums and cholera epidemics until reforms followed Chadwick's 1842 report.
items.misconception: Cities were unhealthy because Victorians did not care about hygiene.
items.random_wrong: Cities were unhealthy because factory smoke poisoned the food.
items.why: Victorians cared deeply but lacked germ theory and assumed disease came from bad air. The real problem was infrastructure, not indifference — leading to landmark public health acts.

items.q: Why did public health reforms accelerate after 1848?
items.correct: The 1848 Public Health Act followed cholera epidemics and Chadwick's report; John Snow's 1854 Broad Street pump study reinforced the case for clean water by 1875.
items.misconception: Public health acts were passed because Victorians discovered germs in the 1840s.
items.random_wrong: The 1875 Act made the NHS free for all workers.
items.why: Germ theory came later (Pasteur, Koch). 1848 reforms were driven by miasma theory plus pragmatic statistics, not bacteriology — students compress the discovery sequence.

items.q: What did the 1832 Great Reform Act actually do?
items.correct: It abolished rotten boroughs, redistributed seats to industrial towns, and extended the vote to about 1 in 5 adult men — still far short of democracy.
items.misconception: The 1832 Reform Act gave the vote to all working men.
items.random_wrong: The 1832 Reform Act allowed women to vote for the first time.
items.why: Working men got the vote only in 1867 (urban) and 1884 (rural). Students compress all 19th-century franchise extensions into one act.

items.q: Why did Chartism fail in the short term?
items.correct: Mass petitions were rejected by Parliament; the movement split between moral-force and physical-force wings; economic improvement after 1848 reduced support.
items.misconception: Chartism failed because no one supported it.
items.random_wrong: Chartism failed because Queen Victoria personally banned the movement.
items.why: Chartism had millions of signatures and was hugely supported. It failed politically, not for lack of support — and five of its six points were eventually adopted.

items.q: When did Britain actually abolish slavery in its empire?
items.correct: Parliament banned the slave trade in 1807 but slavery itself continued until the 1833 Slavery Abolition Act, which took effect in 1834 with compensation paid to slave-owners.
items.misconception: Britain abolished slavery in 1807 thanks to Wilberforce's speech.
items.random_wrong: Britain abolished slavery in 1865 to copy the United States.
items.why: 1807 banned the trade, not slavery. Students conflate the two acts; the 1833 Act famously paid owners (not the enslaved) — a key fact for evaluating motives.

items.q: Why was the Weimar Republic politically unstable?
items.correct: Proportional representation produced fragile coalitions; Article 48 enabled rule by decree; the 'stab-in-the-back' myth delegitimised the republic; extremists attacked from both left and right.
items.misconception: Weimar was unstable because Germans had never experienced democracy before.
items.random_wrong: Weimar was unstable because the Kaiser was still secretly in charge.
items.why: Germany had decades of parliamentary politics under the Kaiserreich. Weimar's problems were structural and contingent, not cultural incapacity for democracy.

items.q: Was the 1923 Munich Putsch a complete failure for Hitler?
items.correct: Militarily yes — it collapsed in hours — but the trial gave Hitler a national platform and he wrote Mein Kampf in prison, learning to pursue power legally instead.
items.misconception: The Munich Putsch destroyed the Nazi Party until Hitler rebuilt it from nothing.
items.random_wrong: The Munich Putsch succeeded in overthrowing the Bavarian government for several weeks.
items.why: The Nazis were small but the putsch was a propaganda gain. Students treat it as straightforward failure; in fact the lessons learned shaped the legal route to 1933.

items.q: What was the political significance of the Reichstag Fire?
items.correct: Hitler used it to issue the Reichstag Fire Decree suspending civil liberties, jail Communists, and create the conditions for the Enabling Act of March 1933.
items.misconception: Hitler personally set the fire and blamed it on the Communists.
items.random_wrong: The fire destroyed all of Berlin and forced people to support the Nazis.
items.why: Historians still debate who started it; van der Lubbe likely acted alone. What matters is how Hitler exploited it — a key step in the legal slide to dictatorship.

items.q: Why did Hitler order the Night of the Long Knives in 1934?
items.correct: To eliminate Ernst Röhm and the SA leadership, reassure the army of his authority, and remove rivals — securing the army's loyalty when Hindenburg died weeks later.
items.misconception: Hitler purged the SA because they were Communists.
items.random_wrong: It was a battle in which the SA tried to overthrow Hitler.
items.why: The SA were Nazis, not Communists. Students miss that this was internal consolidation: Hitler traded the SA for army backing, completing the move to total power.

items.q: How effective was Nazi propaganda in winning popular support?
items.correct: Most effective where it reinforced existing views (anti-Versailles, fear of communism, national pride); less so when contradicting daily experience or established religious belief.
items.misconception: Nazi propaganda was so powerful that Germans believed whatever Hitler said.
items.random_wrong: Goebbels invented television specifically to broadcast Nazi propaganda.
items.why: Propaganda works with the grain of existing beliefs. Students imagine Germans as brainwashed; the reality was selective consent reinforced by terror against dissent.

items.q: What did the 1935 Nuremberg Laws do?
items.correct: They stripped German Jews of citizenship and banned marriage or sexual relations between Jews and 'Aryans', defining Jewishness racially rather than religiously.
items.misconception: The Nuremberg Laws sent Jews directly to concentration camps.
items.random_wrong: The Nuremberg Laws were the post-war trial of Nazi leaders.
items.why: The trials are the Nuremberg Trials (1945-46). The 1935 laws were legal exclusion, a step in escalating persecution — but mass killing came years later.

items.q: When did Nazi Germany move to a total war economy?
items.correct: Only after Stalingrad in 1943, under Speer; before that Hitler had avoided full mobilisation to maintain civilian morale and consumer goods.
items.misconception: Germany was on a total war footing from 1939 onwards.
items.random_wrong: Germany never moved to a war economy because Hitler thought he would win quickly.
items.why: Germany famously delayed full mobilisation, expecting short wars. Students assume dictatorship meant immediate total war; in fact Hitler feared domestic unrest.

items.q: Was the 1905 Revolution a failure?
items.correct: It failed to overthrow the Tsar but forced the October Manifesto creating the Duma, exposing autocracy's weaknesses and rehearsing the structures of 1917.
items.misconception: The 1905 Revolution achieved nothing because the Tsar stayed in power.
items.random_wrong: The 1905 Revolution was led by Lenin from the Winter Palace.
items.why: Lenin was in exile during 1905; the leadership was diverse. Trotsky called it 'the dress rehearsal' — failure in 1905 created conditions for 1917.

items.q: What did Lenin's April Theses demand in 1917?
items.correct: No support for the Provisional Government, all power to the Soviets, end the war, redistribute land — slogans summarised as 'Peace, Bread, Land'.
items.misconception: Lenin's April Theses called for an immediate alliance with the Provisional Government.
items.random_wrong: The April Theses created the Soviet Union.
items.why: Lenin uniquely demanded the Bolsheviks reject the Provisional Government when others wanted cooperation. Students miss how radical this position was even among Bolsheviks.

items.q: How did the Bolsheviks seize power in October 1917?
items.correct: Trotsky's Military Revolutionary Committee coordinated a near-bloodless takeover of key buildings in Petrograd from a Provisional Government that had lost army and worker support.
items.misconception: The October Revolution was a mass popular uprising that stormed the Winter Palace.
items.random_wrong: The October Revolution overthrew the Tsar.
items.why: It was a coordinated coup with limited fighting; the Tsar had been gone since February. Soviet propaganda mythologised it as a mass storming, which film cemented.

items.q: Why did Lenin introduce the NEP in 1921?
items.correct: War Communism had collapsed the economy and provoked the Kronstadt revolt; the NEP allowed limited private trade to restore production and stabilise Bolshevik rule.
items.misconception: The NEP was Lenin's plan to permanently switch Russia to capitalism.
items.random_wrong: The NEP was introduced by Stalin to fund the Five-Year Plans.
items.why: Lenin called the NEP a strategic retreat, not a destination. Students miss the tension between socialist ideology and pragmatic recovery — central to understanding 1920s USSR.

items.q: Was Stalin Lenin's chosen successor?
items.correct: No — Lenin's Testament (1922-23) warned against Stalin's rudeness and recommended his removal; Stalin won by manoeuvring against Trotsky after Lenin's death.
items.misconception: Stalin took power because Lenin nominated him as his successor.
items.random_wrong: Stalin took power by leading the Red Army to victory in the Civil War.
items.why: The Testament was suppressed by the Politburo. Students assume orderly succession; in fact Stalin used his control of party machinery to defeat better-known rivals like Trotsky.

items.q: How successful were Stalin's Five Year Plans?
items.correct: Heavy industry and steel output grew dramatically; consumer goods, quality and worker conditions lagged badly; statistics were inflated, but the USSR became an industrial power.
items.misconception: The Five Year Plans turned the USSR into the most prosperous country in the world.
items.random_wrong: The Five Year Plans were Lenin's last great achievement.
items.why: Living standards stayed low; growth was real but uneven. Students treat Soviet statistics at face value, missing the cost in human welfare and inflated figures.

items.q: Why did Stalin force collectivisation from 1929?
items.correct: To fund industrialisation by extracting grain, eliminate independent kulak farmers as a political threat, and impose Communist control on the countryside.
items.misconception: Collectivisation was popular with peasants who wanted to share land.
items.random_wrong: Collectivisation was suggested by Trotsky and Stalin opposed it.
items.why: Peasants resisted violently, killing livestock; millions died in famine. Students assume voluntary cooperatives; the reality was a brutal state assault on rural society.

items.q: What did the Truman Doctrine commit the US to in 1947?
items.correct: Supporting free peoples resisting communist takeover — initially Greece and Turkey — formalising the policy of containment articulated by George Kennan.
items.misconception: The Truman Doctrine declared war on the Soviet Union.
items.random_wrong: The Truman Doctrine ordered the dropping of the atomic bomb on Japan.
items.why: Containment was political and economic, not military invasion of the USSR. Students conflate Cold War rhetoric with hot war; Truman's doctrine was a framework for indirect rivalry.

items.q: Why did Stalin reject the Marshall Plan?
items.correct: He saw it as dollar imperialism that would pull Eastern Europe into the US orbit; he set up Comecon as an Eastern bloc alternative and blocked satellite states from joining.
items.misconception: Stalin rejected the Marshall Plan because the USSR did not need money after WWII.
items.random_wrong: Stalin rejected the Marshall Plan because it required countries to fight Germany again.
items.why: The USSR was devastated by war and desperately needed aid. Stalin's refusal was strategic, not economic — accepting US dollars meant accepting US influence.

items.q: Why did Stalin blockade West Berlin in 1948?
items.correct: To force the Western Allies out of Berlin in response to the merging of their zones, introduction of the Deutschmark, and creation of a separate West German state.
items.misconception: Stalin blockaded Berlin because he wanted to invade Western Europe.
items.random_wrong: Stalin blockaded Berlin to stop Nazis escaping to the West.
items.why: The blockade was reactive, not a step toward invasion. Students see all Soviet actions as offensive; in fact Berlin was a defensive move against Western consolidation.

items.q: Why did the superpowers pursue détente in the 1970s?
items.correct: Mutual recognition of nuclear parity, US exhaustion from Vietnam, Soviet economic stagnation, and the cost of the arms race — formalised in SALT and the Helsinki Accords.
items.misconception: Détente ended the Cold War in the 1970s.
items.random_wrong: Détente meant the USSR converted to capitalism.
items.why: Détente managed rivalry, it did not end it; the Cold War froze again under Reagan. Students mistake reduced tension for resolution.

items.q: Did Gorbachev intend to dissolve the Soviet Union?
items.correct: No — he wanted to reform and save Soviet socialism; glasnost and perestroika unleashed forces (nationalism, economic chaos) he could not control.
items.misconception: Gorbachev took power planning to abolish communism and dissolve the USSR.
items.random_wrong: Gorbachev dissolved the USSR by signing a treaty with Ronald Reagan.
items.why: Gorbachev was a committed Communist reformer; the collapse was an unintended consequence. Students project the outcome backwards onto his intentions.

items.q: Why did the Soviet Union collapse in 1991?
items.correct: A combination of economic stagnation, nationalist movements in republics, the failed August 1991 coup, and Gorbachev's reforms accelerating loss of control to Yeltsin.
items.misconception: The USSR collapsed because Reagan outspent it in the arms race.
items.random_wrong: The USSR collapsed because NATO invaded Moscow.
items.why: Reagan's arms spending strained but did not topple the USSR; internal economic and political crisis did. Students inherit a triumphalist US narrative that overstates external pressure.

items.q: Did the Wall Street Crash cause the Great Depression?
items.correct: It triggered it but the underlying causes were over-production, agricultural slump, weak banking regulation, and uneven wealth — the Crash exposed structural problems.
items.misconception: The Wall Street Crash on its own caused the Great Depression.
items.random_wrong: The Wall Street Crash happened because the US joined WWII.
items.why: The 1920s boom hid weaknesses (unequal wealth, fragile banks). The Crash was a symptom and accelerant, not a sole cause — important for understanding the New Deal.

items.q: Did the New Deal end the Great Depression?
items.correct: It eased unemployment and restored confidence but did not end the Depression — WWII spending did. Roosevelt's reforms reshaped American government permanently.
items.misconception: Roosevelt's New Deal ended the Great Depression by 1936.
items.random_wrong: The New Deal was a treaty with Britain to share gold reserves.
items.why: Unemployment stayed high until war mobilisation in 1941. Students credit FDR's programmes with the full recovery; historians stress that war ended the slump.

items.q: Why did Prohibition fail in the United States?
items.correct: Widespread evasion via speakeasies and bootlegging, growth of organised crime, loss of tax revenue, and changing public opinion led to repeal in 1933.
items.misconception: Prohibition failed because most Americans were already alcoholics.
items.random_wrong: Prohibition failed because the President personally banned the police from enforcing it.
items.why: Most Americans drank moderately, not pathologically. Failure came from impracticality of enforcement and organised crime profits — a regulatory story, not a moral one.

items.q: What did Brown v Board of Education decide in 1954?
items.correct: The Supreme Court ruled that 'separate but equal' schools were inherently unequal, overturning Plessy v Ferguson and beginning legal dismantling of school segregation.
items.misconception: Brown v Board of Education immediately desegregated all schools in America.
items.random_wrong: Brown v Board ended slavery in the southern states.
items.why: Implementation was patchy and resisted (e.g. Little Rock 1957). The ruling was a legal foundation, not instant change — students assume court decisions equal social reality.

items.q: How did Malcolm X's approach differ from MLK's?
items.correct: MLK preached nonviolent integration; Malcolm X (until 1964) advocated black self-defence, separatism, and rejected integration as a goal — though both shifted positions over time.
items.misconception: Malcolm X advocated violent terrorism against white Americans.
items.random_wrong: Malcolm X and MLK were close allies who jointly led the March on Washington.
items.why: Malcolm X advocated self-defence, not aggressive terrorism. Students contrast them too sharply; both critiqued American racism, with Malcolm X moderating after his 1964 Hajj.

items.q: How successful was JFK's presidency?
items.correct: Mixed — failed Bay of Pigs and slow civil rights legislation, but Cuban Missile Crisis was handled, and Apollo and Peace Corps proved popular. He was killed before major legislative wins.
items.misconception: Kennedy was the greatest US president because everyone loved him.
items.random_wrong: Kennedy passed the Civil Rights Act before his assassination.
items.why: The Civil Rights Act passed under Johnson in 1964. Kennedy's reputation rests heavily on style and martyrdom; substantive achievements were limited by his short term.

items.q: Why did Nixon resign in 1974?
items.correct: The release of the White House tapes proved he obstructed the FBI's Watergate inquiry; facing certain impeachment by Congress, he resigned to avoid conviction.
items.misconception: Nixon resigned because he personally ordered the Watergate break-in.
items.random_wrong: Nixon resigned because he lost the 1972 election to Carter.
items.why: Nixon's crime was the cover-up, not necessarily ordering the burglary. Students assume worse acts when the constitutional issue was actually obstruction of justice.

items.q: What was the theory of the four humours?
items.correct: Disease was caused by imbalance of blood, phlegm, yellow bile and black bile; treatment aimed to rebalance them via bloodletting, purging or diet. It dominated for nearly 2,000 years.
items.misconception: Medieval doctors knew about germs but used humours because they had no medicine.
items.random_wrong: The four humours were invented by Pasteur to explain bacteria.
items.why: Humoral theory was a coherent system from Hippocrates and Galen, not ignorance. Students retroject germ theory backwards, missing why educated people believed it for so long.

items.q: Why was Vesalius significant in the history of medicine?
items.correct: His 1543 De Humani Corporis Fabrica corrected Galen's errors via human dissection and accurate illustration, founding modern anatomy though not curing disease.
items.misconception: Vesalius discovered the cause of disease and ended humoral theory.
items.random_wrong: Vesalius invented the microscope.
items.why: Vesalius improved anatomy but disease theory still ran on humours for centuries. Students conflate progress in one area (structure) with progress in another (cause of illness).

items.q: Why was Jenner's smallpox vaccination significant in 1796?
items.correct: He showed cowpox material could prevent smallpox safely — the first effective vaccine, though he did not know why it worked; germ theory came nearly a century later.
items.misconception: Jenner discovered germs and used them to make vaccines.
items.random_wrong: Jenner used penicillin to cure smallpox.
items.why: Jenner worked empirically, not from germ theory which was Pasteur's. Students collapse the discoveries together; vaccination predates the science explaining it.

items.q: What was Pasteur's contribution to medicine?
items.correct: He proved germ theory of disease via experiments on fermentation and spontaneous generation, then developed vaccines for rabies and anthrax — Koch identified specific bacteria.
items.misconception: Pasteur invented antibiotics like penicillin.
items.random_wrong: Pasteur invented the stethoscope.
items.why: Penicillin came from Fleming in 1928. Students lump 19th-century discoveries together; Pasteur proved the theory of disease, others built treatments on it later.

items.q: Why was the NHS founded in 1948?
items.correct: Beveridge's 1942 report identified disease as a 'giant evil'; Labour's 1945 manifesto promised universal healthcare; Bevan unified hospitals and made care free at point of use.
items.misconception: The NHS was founded because Churchill wanted to thank soldiers after WWII.
items.random_wrong: The NHS was founded in 1918 after the influenza pandemic.
items.why: Churchill's Conservatives opposed parts of the NHS; it was Labour's Bevan who pushed it through. Students misattribute it to wartime consensus alone, missing the political conflict.

items.q: How was crime punished in Anglo-Saxon England?
items.correct: Through wergild ('man-price') compensation for victims, communal responsibility via tithings, trial by ordeal in disputed cases, and the death penalty for serious crimes.
items.misconception: Anglo-Saxons had no laws and just took revenge on each other.
items.random_wrong: Anglo-Saxons punished all crimes with imprisonment in castle dungeons.
items.why: Castles and prisons as we know them didn't exist; Anglo-Saxon law was sophisticated and codified. Students imagine lawless chaos when in fact community-based justice was elaborate.

items.q: What was the Bloody Code?
items.correct: The 18th-century English legal system that made over 200 offences capital, intended as deterrence, but often softened by jury nullification and transportation to Australia.
items.misconception: The Bloody Code meant everyone caught for any crime was hanged immediately.
items.random_wrong: The Bloody Code was a Tudor law against witchcraft.
items.why: Many sentences were commuted; juries acquitted to avoid hanging for petty theft. Students take the law at face value, missing the gap between statute and actual practice.

items.q: Why did Robert Peel create the Metropolitan Police in 1829?
items.correct: Industrial London's crime, poor watchmen, and fear of disorder pushed Peel to create a professional, uniformed but unarmed force focused on crime prevention.
items.misconception: Peel created the police because there had been no law enforcement of any kind before 1829.
items.random_wrong: Peel created the police to fight the French during the Napoleonic Wars.
items.why: Parish constables and watchmen existed but were inadequate for industrial cities. Students imagine total chaos before 1829, missing the prior patchwork of local enforcement.

items.q: Why were the Whitechapel murders never solved?
items.correct: Lack of forensic techniques, no fingerprinting or DNA, conflicting witness accounts, jurisdictional confusion between forces, and possibly police incompetence and media interference.
items.misconception: The police failed to solve the murders because they didn't try hard enough.
items.random_wrong: The Ripper was caught but his name was kept secret to protect the Royal Family.
items.why: The police investigated heavily but lacked tools we take for granted. Royal conspiracy theories are unsupported; the limitation was technological and institutional.

items.q: How did prison purposes change in the 19th century?
items.correct: Prisons shifted from holding before trial or transportation to punishment and reform via the Separate and Silent systems, influenced by Fry and the 1823 and 1865 Prison Acts.
items.misconception: Victorian prisons were created to rehabilitate prisoners kindly.
items.random_wrong: Victorian prisons abolished hard labour and corporal punishment.
items.why: Reform meant solitary confinement and hard labour intended to break the will, not modern rehabilitation. Students project 21st-century welfare values onto Victorian discipline.

items.q: How did Anglo-Saxons come to dominate post-Roman Britain?
items.correct: A mix of migration from northern Germany and Denmark, settlement and intermarriage, and cultural absorption of Romano-British populations — not simple invasion and replacement.
items.misconception: Anglo-Saxons invaded Britain and wiped out all the Romano-British inhabitants.
items.random_wrong: Anglo-Saxons came as refugees fleeing the Vikings.
items.why: Genetic and archaeological evidence shows mixed populations and continuity. The 'extermination' model is an old textbook trope replaced by migration-and-assimilation.

items.q: Why were Jews expelled from England in 1290?
items.correct: Edward I expelled Jews after a century of restrictions and taxation had impoverished the community; antisemitism, debts owed to Jews, and political pressure all contributed.
items.misconception: Jews left England voluntarily because they were no longer welcome.
items.random_wrong: Jews were expelled because they refused to fight in the Crusades.
items.why: Expulsion was a royal edict, not voluntary. Students sometimes assume migrations are always chosen; medieval Jewish history is a story of state coercion.

items.q: Why did Huguenots migrate to England from the 1680s?
items.correct: Louis XIV's 1685 revocation of the Edict of Nantes ended toleration of French Protestants; many fled persecution to Protestant England, bringing skills like silk weaving.
items.misconception: Huguenots came to England because France was poorer than England.
items.random_wrong: Huguenots were Catholic refugees fleeing Cromwell.
items.why: Huguenots were Protestants fleeing Catholic persecution, not economic migrants. Students project modern migration motives onto religious refugees.

items.q: Why did Caribbean migrants come to Britain in 1948 on the Windrush?
items.correct: Britain faced post-war labour shortages; the 1948 British Nationality Act gave Commonwealth citizens UK residency rights and Caribbean migrants answered the call to rebuild.
items.misconception: Windrush migrants were illegal immigrants from outside the empire.
items.random_wrong: Windrush migrants came as refugees from Caribbean famines.
items.why: They arrived as British subjects with full legal rights — the irony of the modern 'Windrush scandal'. Students miss the citizenship framework that made the migration unproblematic in law.

items.q: Why did Britain restrict Commonwealth immigration from 1962?
items.correct: Rising public hostility, political pressure, and economic concerns led to the 1962 Commonwealth Immigrants Act introducing voucher controls, followed by tighter restrictions in 1968 and 1971.
items.misconception: Britain restricted Commonwealth immigration because there was no work available.
items.random_wrong: Britain restricted immigration because the Commonwealth ceased to exist in 1962.
items.why: There was labour demand but political backlash drove restriction. Students assume economics drives policy; in fact race politics and public opinion were decisive.

items.q: Why did the Schlieffen Plan fail in 1914?
items.correct: Belgian resistance delayed the German advance; Russia mobilised faster than expected; Moltke weakened the right wing; and the French rallied at the Marne.
items.misconception: The Schlieffen Plan failed because Britain entered the war.
items.random_wrong: The Schlieffen Plan failed because it tried to invade Russia first.
items.why: Britain's army was small in 1914; failure was about timing and logistics, not British intervention. Students overestimate the BEF's role in the initial defeat.

items.q: Why did the July Crisis of 1914 escalate to world war?
items.correct: Germany's 'blank cheque' encouraged Austria's ultimatum to Serbia; Russian mobilisation triggered German plans; rigid railway timetables and the alliance system locked great powers in.
items.misconception: The July Crisis escalated because every country wanted a big war.
items.random_wrong: The July Crisis escalated because the Kaiser was personally insane.
items.why: Leaders feared war but felt trapped by alliance commitments and mobilisation timetables. Students assume eagerness; in fact stumbling into war is a key historiographical concept.

items.q: Why did the League of Nations fail to prevent WWII?
items.correct: Absence of the US, requirement of unanimous decisions, no armed force of its own, weak responses to Manchuria (1931) and Abyssinia (1935), and great-power self-interest.
items.misconception: The League failed because no countries joined it.
items.random_wrong: The League failed because Hitler banned it in 1933.
items.why: Many countries joined; the failure was structural and political, not absence. Students reduce complex institutional failure to a single fact.

items.q: Did the Treaty of Versailles directly cause WWII?
items.correct: It contributed via German resentment and economic strain, but the 1930s Depression, Hitler's choices, and Allied appeasement were equally necessary — Versailles alone is not sufficient.
items.misconception: Versailles caused WWII directly because it was so harsh on Germany.
items.random_wrong: Versailles caused WWII because it required Germany to invade Poland.
items.why: Comparable peace terms have not always led to war. Modern historians stress contingency; treating Versailles as sufficient cause is a simple but inadequate story.

items.q: Did suffragette militancy help or hinder votes for women?
items.correct: Historians disagree — militancy raised the issue's profile but also alienated MPs; the suspension of activism in WWI may have done more to enable 1918 reform.
items.misconception: Suffragette militancy is the main reason women got the vote in 1918.
items.random_wrong: Emmeline Pankhurst was the first woman to be elected Prime Minister.
items.why: Militancy may have hardened opposition; constitutional suffragists (Fawcett) often did more practical work. Students attribute victory to the most dramatic group, ignoring complexity.

items.q: How did trade unions become legal in 19th-century Britain?
items.correct: Combination Acts banned them until 1824-25 partial repeal; the 1834 Tolpuddle case galvanised opinion; full legal recognition came via Acts in 1871 and 1875.
items.misconception: Trade unions were legalised by Tolpuddle Martyrs being acquitted.
items.random_wrong: Trade unions were legalised by the 1832 Reform Act.
items.why: The Tolpuddle men were transported; legalisation took decades of pressure. Students compress union history into a single dramatic case rather than the long legislative process.

items.q: What did the 1928 Equal Franchise Act do?
items.correct: It gave women the vote on the same terms as men — all adults over 21 — completing the process begun in 1918, which had only enfranchised women over 30 with property.
items.misconception: The 1918 Representation of the People Act gave all women the vote.
items.random_wrong: The 1928 Act gave women in Britain the vote for the first time.
items.why: 1918 enfranchised older, propertied women; 1928 completed equal franchise. Students collapse the two acts into one, missing the decade of partial enfranchisement.

items.q: Why is Magna Carta still cited in 'Power and the People' history?
items.correct: Because clauses on lawful judgement and protection from arbitrary imprisonment were reinterpreted in the 17th century to support broader liberties, even though they originally protected only barons.
items.misconception: Magna Carta directly created modern human rights in 1215.
items.random_wrong: Magna Carta gave the right to vote to all English men.
items.why: Its symbolic power grew over time, especially during the 17th-century constitutional struggles. Students confuse historical use with original meaning — a key historiographical insight.

items.q: Why was the founding of the NHS controversial in 1948?
items.correct: The British Medical Association opposed nationalisation of doctors; Bevan made concessions allowing private practice; Conservatives feared cost and central control.
items.misconception: The NHS was supported by all parties and all doctors from the start.
items.random_wrong: The NHS was originally only for soldiers returning from WWII.
items.why: Bevan said he had to 'stuff their mouths with gold' to get doctors on board. Students project today's cross-party support backwards onto a deeply contested founding.

items.q: What was the Tet Offensive's main significance?
items.correct: Militarily the Viet Cong suffered heavy losses, but politically it shattered the US public belief that the war was being won, fuelling anti-war pressure on Johnson.
items.misconception: The Tet Offensive was a military victory for the Viet Cong that drove out the US.
items.random_wrong: The Tet Offensive ended the Vietnam War in 1968.
items.why: Militarily it was a US tactical win but a strategic political loss. Students confuse battlefield outcomes with political effects — vital for understanding insurgency warfare.

items.q: Why did Khrushchev place missiles in Cuba in 1962?
items.correct: To deter US invasion of Cuba, redress strategic missile imbalance (the US had missiles in Turkey), and bolster Soviet bargaining power — not as a first step in invading the US.
items.misconception: Khrushchev placed missiles in Cuba to prepare for a nuclear first strike on the US.
items.random_wrong: Khrushchev placed missiles in Cuba because Castro asked him to invade Florida.
items.why: The deployment was strategic and defensive of Cuba. Students assume aggression; in fact Khrushchev sought parity and protection of an ally — leading to the negotiated resolution.

items.q: How did Hitler legally become dictator in March 1933?
items.correct: The Enabling Act passed with Centre Party support after KPD deputies were arrested or fled; it let Hitler legislate without the Reichstag for four years.
items.misconception: Hitler made himself dictator immediately after becoming Chancellor in January 1933.
items.random_wrong: Hitler was declared dictator by a national referendum.
items.why: It took weeks of constitutional manoeuvring and the Reichstag Fire to engineer. Students collapse 'Hitler took power' into a single moment, missing the legal process.

items.q: Why did the Provisional Government fail in 1917?
items.correct: It continued the unpopular war, did not redistribute land, shared 'dual power' with the Petrograd Soviet, and was discredited by the Kornilov affair before the Bolshevik takeover.
items.misconception: The Provisional Government fell because Lenin was personally more charismatic than Kerensky.
items.random_wrong: The Provisional Government fell because the Tsar returned and reclaimed his throne.
items.why: Its failure was structural, not personal: war, land and dual power. Students reduce political collapse to charismatic leadership when material grievances drove events.

items.q: Did the Battle of Bosworth in 1485 end the Wars of the Roses?
items.correct: It killed Richard III and put Henry Tudor on the throne, but Yorkist threats (Simnel 1487, Warbeck 1490s) continued — the wars only truly ended with Stoke Field in 1487.
items.misconception: Bosworth in 1485 ended the Wars of the Roses immediately.
items.random_wrong: Bosworth was a naval battle fought off the south coast of England.
items.why: Henry VII faced rebellions for over a decade. Students learn 1485 as a tidy endpoint; in fact securing the dynasty took further fighting and ruthless control of pretenders.

items.q: Why were railways transformative for Victorian Britain?
items.correct: They cut transport costs and times, integrated national markets, enabled commuting and mass leisure travel, and standardised time zones — reshaping economy and society after 1830.
items.misconception: Railways were transformative mainly because they were faster than horses.
items.random_wrong: Railways were transformative because they were the first form of public transport.
items.why: Speed mattered but the deeper change was economic integration: prices converged, goods moved, towns grew around lines. Students focus on novelty rather than systemic impact.

items.q: What did the Wannsee Conference of January 1942 decide?
items.correct: It coordinated the bureaucracy of the already-decided Final Solution across ministries, scaling existing mass murder into a systematic Europe-wide extermination programme.
items.misconception: Wannsee was the meeting where the Nazis first decided to murder all Jews.
items.random_wrong: Wannsee was the meeting where Hitler personally launched WWII.
items.why: Mass shootings had begun in 1941; the decision predates Wannsee. The conference organised what was already happening — students treat it as the starting point when it was a coordination meeting.

items.q: Why was the Berlin Wall built in 1961?
items.correct: To stop the mass flow of East Germans to the West via Berlin (millions had fled), which was draining the GDR of skilled workers and threatening the regime's survival.
items.misconception: The Berlin Wall was built to stop a Western invasion of East Berlin.
items.random_wrong: The Berlin Wall was built by NATO to keep East Germans out of West Germany.
items.why: The Wall faced inward to trap GDR citizens, not outward against invasion. Students miss that it was an admission of weakness — people were voting with their feet.

items.q: Was Rosa Parks's refusal to give up her bus seat a spontaneous act?
items.correct: No — Parks was a trained NAACP activist; the boycott was organised by long-standing civil rights networks that turned her arrest into a planned campaign.
items.misconception: Rosa Parks was just a tired seamstress who refused on impulse to give up her seat.
items.random_wrong: Rosa Parks was the lawyer who argued Brown v Board.
items.why: The 'tired seamstress' story was crafted for public sympathy. Students inherit a depoliticised version that obscures organised civil rights activism.

items.q: Why did Luther's 95 Theses spread so quickly in 1517?
items.correct: The printing press allowed rapid reproduction; existing critique of Church corruption and indulgence sales found ready audiences; German princes had political reasons to back Luther against Rome.
items.misconception: Luther's ideas spread because he was the first to criticise the Catholic Church.
items.random_wrong: Luther's ideas spread because the Pope endorsed them.
items.why: Critics like Hus and Wycliffe predated Luther by over a century. The printing press and political conditions are what made Luther succeed where earlier reformers failed.

items.q: How real was the Weimar 'Golden Age' under Stresemann?
items.correct: Recovery was real but fragile — funded by short-term US loans that vanished after 1929; political extremism declined but did not disappear; cultural flowering masked unresolved tensions.
items.misconception: Under Stresemann the Weimar Republic was completely stable and prosperous.
items.random_wrong: Stresemann ruled as dictator from 1923 to 1929.
items.why: Loans made prosperity contingent on US economy; Nazis still scored 6.5% in 1924. Students treat the period as solid, missing the structural fragility that 1929 exposed.

items.q: Who were the main victims of the Great Terror?
items.correct: Old Bolsheviks, the military officer corps, ethnic minorities, kulaks, ordinary citizens denounced by neighbours, and eventually the NKVD itself — millions arrested or shot, 1936-38.
items.misconception: Stalin only purged senior Communist Party rivals.
items.random_wrong: Stalin only purged Jews and Ukrainians.
items.why: The Terror cut across all social groups, with mass operations targeting ordinary people. Students focus on the show trials and miss the much larger ordinary terror.
